ORIGINAL PAPER


# Copper(II) ions enhance the peroxidase-like activity and stability of keratin-capped gold nanoclusters for the colorimetric detection of glucose


Shuyi Ma1 & Jinjie Wang1 & Guang Yang2 & Jingxia Yang1 & Derun Ding1 & Min Zhang1

Received: 14December2018 /Accepted: 29March2019 # Springer-Verlag GmbH Austria, part of Springer Nature2019


# Abstract


Amethod is described for the preparation of copper(II)-modified keratin-capped gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) with adjustable Au/ Cu molar ratio through a two-step synthetic route. The introduction of Cu(II) is known to cause quenching of the fluorescence of such AuNCs. It is found, however, that the Cu(II) loaded AuNC (AuNC-Cu2+ ) display strongly enhanced peroxidase-like activity and improved chemical stability. This is assumed to be due to the synergistic effect of the gold and copper atoms and in contrast to the single components (pure AuNCs and copper ions). The kinetic parameters of the new peroxidase mimic show a higher K cat value (12.1 × 10 -4s -1 ) and a lower Km value (53 μ M) for H2O2 (compared to those of conventional AuNCs). The catalytic activity is stable and remains essentially unchanged after two months. The interactions of AuNCs with Cu(II) were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, UV-vis spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Based on these findings, a glucose colorimetric assay at452nm was developed that has a detection range from1.6to800 μ Mand a0.26 μ Mdetection limit.

Keywords Copper ion modification . Synergistic activity . Enzyme mimic . Catalyst . Glucose assay


# Introduction


Natural enzymes have been widely applied in various fields including the chemical industry, clinical medicine, food processing and agricultural areas, due to their strong catalytic ability [1]. Peroxidase, which can activate hydrogen

Shuyi Ma and Jinjie Wang contributed equally to this work.

Electronic supplementary material The online version of this article (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604-019-3395-8) contains supplementary material, which is available to authorized users.

- Jinjie Wang jinjiewang@sues.edu.cn

- Min Zhang zhangmin@sues.edu.cn

- 1College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science, 333Longteng Road, Shanghai201620, People ' s Republic of China

- 2College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, 2999North Renmin Road, Shanghai201620, People ' s Republic of China

peroxide (H2O2) to perform various chemical reactions, has been extensively studied and broadly used [2, 3]. Various artificial enzyme mimics with favorable catalytic properties also have been fabricated. Nanomaterials, such as metal oxides [4], metal nanoparticles or nanoclusters [5, 6], metal-organic frameworks [7] and carbon nanomaterials [8], have been found to possess peroxidase-like activity. These nanomaterials are well suited for bioanalysis as peroxidase mimics due to their small size, high specific surface area, excellent chemical stability and biocompatibility [9]. Among these materials, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are catalysts with great potential in the field of bioanalysis.

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) with peroxidase-like activity have been used to develop several applications in chemical sensing and biosensing [10 -13]. However, compared with the number of studies on their fluorescent properties [14 -16], the number of studies on the peroxidaselike catalytic properties of AuNCs is relatively limited. Similar to most nanozymes, the AuNC-based peroxidasemimicking system suffers from shortcomings, such as decreased catalytic activity, unstable catalytic properties and strong acidic reaction conditions (approximately pH4) [17 -19]. Methods for improving the catalytic activity of nanomaterials have been reported, including controlling

1

2

7

the ligands, morphology, and composition of materials [20 -22]. Similar approaches have been reported for improving the catalytic activity of AuNCs. For example, the Qu and Ren group synthesized hybrid graphene oxide (GO)-AuNCs by absorbing AuNCs onto GO through simple electrostatic interactions, and this hybrid exhibited excellent peroxidase-like activity [23]. Wu and Huang synthesized bimetallic Au-PtNCs with enhanced peroxidase-like activity by the direct co-reduction of two metallic precursors using glutathione as both a reducing and protective agent [24]. Hu ' s group found that heparin greatly accelerated the peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs, with a25-fold increase at a neutral pH [2]. Nevertheless, it is still challenging to easily synthesize AuNC nanozymes with a stable and high catalytic activity.

In this study, a method is described to enhance the peroxidase-like activity and stability of keratin-capped AuNCs by using copper ions, this approach was inspired by the following two aspects. First, many studies have proven that the properties of gold nanomaterials such as luminescence [25, 26], electrochemistry [6, 20] and catalysis [27, 28] are strongly influenced by Au-Cu interactions. Nosaka et al. found that the photocatalytic performance of AuNPs-TiO2photocatalysts was improved significantly by loading the particles with Cu2+ ions [27]. Chen ' s group reported a highly sensitive colorimetric determination of Hg2+ using a Cu@AuNPs3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) system, and the sensitivity was much lower than that of a previously established AuNP-based colorimetric assay [28]. Second, Willner ' s group successfully synthesized a series of Cu2+ ion-modified graphene oxide nanoparticles, metal-organic framework nanoparticles, carbon nitride nanoparticles and carbon dots, and found that these Cu2+ ion-modified nanomaterials are good catalysts [29 -31]. Therefore, it is feasible to increase the catalytic activity of AuNCs by using copper ions. The experimental results show that compared with pure AuNCs, AuNC-Cu2+ not only have more significant peroxidasel i k e activity, but also have excellent stability. Furthermore, the interactions of AuNCs with Cu2+ were characterized by UV-vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The synthesized AuNC-Cu2+ showed a lower detection limit than that of AuNCs for the colorimetric detection of H2O2and glucose. Glucose, as an important metabolite for living organisms, is a crucial indicator for the diagnosis of diabetes [32]. This system, using AuNC-Cu2+ as a catalyst, exhibited high sensitivity along with good reproducibility toward glucose, thus showing great potential for future use as a clinical diagnostic platform.


# Experimental section



# Materials


Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.9%) and3,3 ′ 5,5 ' -Tetramethylbenzldine (TMB) were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co.Ltd. (China, http://www.aladdin-e.com/). Glucose oxidase was supplied by BBI Life Sciences (Canada, http://bbi-lifesciences.com/). H2O2was obtained from LingFeng Chemical Reagent Co.Ltd. (China, http:// www.yonghuachem.com/). Keratins were prepared by reductive extraction using feather according to our previously published method [15]. Duck feather were supplied by SleepHometex Co.Ltd. (China, http://www. sleephometex.com/). Three human sera samples were provided by Hospital of Shanghai Jiaotong University.


# Preparation of gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and AuNC-Cu2+


Keratin-capped AuNCs were synthesized by modifying a previously method [15]. To obtain AuNCs with high peroxidaselike activity, different concentrations of keratin and different synthesis times were optimized; the results are shown in Fig. S1. Considering the peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs and the interference of the background signal, 15mg·mL -1keratin with5mM HAuCl4was selected as the suitable reagent concentration and14h was selected as the synthesis time.

AuNC-Cu2+ were prepared by simply mixing AuNCs with Cu2+ ions. First, AuNCs were purified by filtering with a Sephadex G25 (Sweden, http://www.gelifesciences.com/) size exclusion column (SEC). Second, 1mL of a purified AuNC solution (2mg·mL -1 ) was added to1mL of copper sulfate (0 -444 μ M) at room temperature and allowed to react for6h with gentle stirring. Finally, AuNC-Cu2+ were stored at4 °C for further use.


# Peroxidase-like activity based assays


H2O2detection was performed according to the following procedures: I) 150 μ L of AuNC-Cu2+ (3mg·mL -1 ), 9 μ L of TMB (10mg·mL -1 ) and30 μ L of different concentrations of H2O2were mixed with711 μ L Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH4.0) sequentially. The mixed solution was then incubated in a shaking air incubator at40 °C for30min. II) The reaction solution was centrifuged at10,000rpm for1min, and then the precipitate at the bottom of the tube was retained. Since the pH of the reaction system is near the isoelectric point of keratin, the keratin adsorbed by the one-electron oxidation intermediate TMB (ox1TMB) in the catalytic reaction is more unstable and precipitates. III) Then, 300 μ L of2M H2SO4was added

to dissolve the ox1-TMB adsorbed on the keratin, and the ox1-TMB further lost an electron to form yellow ox2-TMB under acidic conditions. The absorbance of the supernatant (obtained after centrifugation at10,000rpm for1min) at452nm was measured on a UV-1601PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Fig. S2a shows photographs of H2O2detection process. This process involved two steps of centrifugation operations, which is slightly more complicated than other methods. However, this operation concentrated the concentration of yellow ox2-TMB, which could increase the sensitivity and accuracy of this assay. Furthermore, to compare the effect of the reaction solution pH on AuNC-Cu2+ , BR buffer from pH2.0to12.0were studied at40 °C. To examine the effect of different temperatures on the AuNC-Cu2+ , the catalytic reactions incubated at different temperatures from25to45 °C were studied at pH4.0. For temperature optimization, the absorbance data were determined by a VERSAmax microplate reader (Molecular Devices, UK) at a wavelength of650nm.


# Kinetic analysis


Kinetic studies were performed by monitoring changes in absorbance at a wavelength of452nm. The Michaelis-Menten constant was calculated using the double reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plots of the Michaelis-Menten equation. The experiments were carried out with0.5mg·mL -1AuNCs or AuNC-Cu2+ and0.1mg·mL -1TMBin BR buffer (pH4.0) when H2O2was used as the substrate. The concentration of H2O2was adjusted to12.5, 25, 50, 100and200 μ M. Similarly, when TMB was used as a substrate, catalytic experiments were carried out with0.5mg·mL -1AuNCs or AuNCCu2+ and100 μ MH2O2 (3mM) in BR buffer (pH4.0). The concentration of TMB was adjusted to0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6and0.7mM.


# Glucose detection


The detection of glucose was performed according to H2O2detection methods with slight modifications as follows: 150 μ L of AuNC-Cu2+ (3mg·mL -1 ), 30 μ L of GOx (60mg·mL -1 ), 9 μ L of TMB (10mg·mL -1 ) and30 μ L of glucose with different concentrations were mixed with681 μ L BR buffer (pH4.0) sequentially. The mixed solution was then incubated for60min in a shaking air incubator at37 °C. The subsequent steps were the same as those for H2O2detection. Fig. S2b shows photographs of glucose detection process. Furthermore, to compare the effect of the reaction solution pH on AuNC-Cu2+ , BR buffer from pH3.0to5.5were studied at37 °C. As shown in Fig. S3, pH4.0was the optimum pH for the reaction.


# Results and discussion



# Choice of materials


Many nanomaterials, such as metal oxides, metal nanoparticles or nanoclusters, metal-organic frameworks and carbon nanomaterials are well suited for bioanalysis as peroxidase mimics. Among these materials, AuNCs are catalysts with great potential in the field of bioanalysis due to their small size and biocompatibility. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a method for synthesizing AuNC nanozymes with a stable and high catalytic activity.


# Peroxidase-like activity of AuNC-Cu2+


Our group has successfully prepared keratin-capped AuNCs with peroxidase-like activity that can catalyze the oxidation of a TMB peroxidase substrate with H2O2. However, the peroxidase-like activity of unpurified AuNCs was not stable (Fig. S4). To further improve the stability and intensity of AuNC peroxidase-like activity, we proposed a strategy in which the AuNCs were modified with Cu2+ ions. As evidenced by many previous studies, Cu2+ can quench the fluorescence of AuNCs [25, 33]. In this work, we were surprised to find that copper ions can greatly promote the catalytic activity and stability of AuNCs. The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of AuNCs was studied in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 1). In the absence of Cu2+ ions, the color of the solution was light yellow indicating that the catalytic activity of AuNCs was low (system3). Interestingly, a noticeable yellow color of the solution after the addition of Cu2+ ions into the mixture (system4) was observed indicating that the catalytic activity of AuNCs was greatly enhanced. The control

Fig. 1The absorbance at452nm and photographs of the color evolution (inset) in different reaction systems. (1) Keratin, TMB and H2O2; (2) keratin, Cu2+ , TMB and H2O2; (3) AuNCs, TMB and H2O2; (4) AuNCs, Cu2+ , TMB and H2O2. Reaction conditions: keratin, 0.5mg·mL -1 ; Cu2+ , 111 μ M; AuNCs, 0.5mg·mL -1 ; TMB, 0.1mg·mL -1 ; H2O2, 100 μ M; BR buffer (pH4.0)

experiment was performed by adding Cu2+ ions to a mixture of keratin, TMB and H2O2. The solution color of system2was nearly colorless indicating that Cu2+ ions showed negligible catalytic activity in this experimental system.

The peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs after modification with different concentrations of Cu2+ ions was studied. The peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs purified by SEC was greatly improved with increasing Cu2+ ion concentrations (Fig. 2a). When the Cu2+ ion concentration was111 μ M, AuNCs with high catalytic activity and low background signal were obtained. The fluorescence spectra of AuNCs mixed with different concentrations of Cu2+ ions were measured by an Edinburgh FS-5fluorescence spectrometer (Fig. 2b). When the Cu2+ ion concentration was greater than111 μ M, the fluorescence intensity of most of the AuNCs was quenched, indicating that most of the AuNCs interacted with Cu2+ ions. Hence, 111 μ M CuSO4corresponding to1mg·mL -1AuNCs(approximately8copper atoms per gold cluster) was selected as the optimal reaction concentration. Subsequently, the stability of the AuNC-Cu2+ peroxidase-like activity was investigated for practical applications. Figure2c shows the effect of storage time on the peroxidase-like activity of AuNC-Cu2+ in the absence and presence of100 μ M H2O2. Clearly, almost no changes were observed after storage for2months. All these data suggest that a simple mixture of AuNCs and Cu2+ ions is an effective method to enhance the stability and intensity of the peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs.


# Characterization of AuNC-Cu2+


The structure of AuNC-Cu2+ was investigated by by TEM (JEOL JEM-2100), UV-vis absorption spectroscopy (Shimadzu UV-1601PC), fluorescence spectroscopy (Edinburgh FS-5), XRD (Shimadzu D/Max-2500) and XPS (Physical Electronics PHI5600). No significant differences were observed in the TEM images (Fig. S5a and5b), UV spectra (Fig. S5c) or XRD patterns (Fig. S6), while the fluorescence signal (Fig. S5d) showed a large difference. These data suggest that the interaction between AuNCs and Cu2+

ions was mainly focused on the interactions between Au and Cu atoms.

To further investigate the interactions between AuNCs and copper ions, the chemical states of Au and Cu in the samples was obtained by XPS (Fig. 3, S7and S8). The Au4f7/2spectrum can be fitted to two peaks: 84.1and84.9eV , corresponding to Au0and Au + , respectively [15]. Unexpectedly, after adding Cu2+ ions, almost all of the Au in AuNC-Cu2+ was in a reduced state (Au0 ). The disappearance of Au + may be due to the formation of an Au-Cu alloy or Cu + (d10 )-Au + (d10 ) metallophilic bonds [34]. Furthermore, the XPS peak of932.8eV for the Cu2p3/2electron indicated that the copper atoms in AuNC-Cu2+ exist in Cu + or Cu0valance states [35]. The absence of the peak at942eVreflects the absence of Cu2+ in AuNC-Cu2+ . To verify whether the enhanced peroxidaselike activity of AuNC-Cu2+ was due to the coordination of Cu + with Au + or the formation of an Au-Cu alloy, ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) was used as a chelator agent to compete with AuNCs for Cu. As shown in Fig. S9a, the peroxidase-like activity of AuNC-Cu2+ was greatly reduced when EDTA was introduced, indicating that the interaction between Cu and AuNCs was through Cu + (d10 )-Au + (d10 ) coordination.

In summary, the AuNC-Cu2+ display strongly enhanced peroxidase-like activity in contrast to pure AuNCs and copper ions. The synergistic effect of the gold and copper atoms was through Cu + (d10 )-Au + (d10 ) coordination, which was evidenced by the XPS data. In addition, the experimental results also proved that the peroxidase-like activity of AuNC-Cu2+ was dependent on oxygen or hydrogen peroxide as oxidants. The catalytic activity of AuNC-Cu2+ further decreased after bubbling pure nitrogen into the reaction solution to eliminate dissolved oxygen (Fig. S9b).


# Steady-state kinetics of AuNCs and AuNC-Cu2+


The steady-state kinetics of the peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs and AuNC-Cu2+ were investigated (Fig. S10), and the enzyme kinetic parameters are shown in Table S1. The

Michaelis-Menten constant was calculated by double reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plots of the Michaelis-Menten equation: 1/ v0 = Km/ Vmax(1/[ S ] + 1/ Km), where v0is the initial velocity, Km is the Michaelis constant, Vmax is the maximum reaction velocity and [ S ] is the concentration of the substrate. Typically, a lower Km value indicates a greater affinity between the enzyme and substrate. A higher Vmax value indicates that the system has a larger range where the response is proportional to the substrate concentration allowing for reliable quantification of the analyte. As shown in Table S1, the Km value of AuNC-Cu2+ was lower than that of AuNCs and the Vmax was higher than that of AuNCs with H2O2as the substrate, which indicated that AuNC-Cu2+ have a higher affinity and larger detection range for H2O2. The catalytic efficiency of AuNC-Cu2+ was3.3-fold greater than that of AuNCs. However, compared to AuNCs, AuNC-Cu2+ had a lower catalytic efficiency with TMB as a substrate.

# Optimization of peroxidase-like reaction conditions


The catalytic activity of AuNC-Cu2+ depends on the H2O2concentration. This implies that the change in absorbance at452nm can be used to determine H2O2. To improve the detection sensitivity of H2O2, the effects of the reaction solution pH and reaction temperature on the catalytic activity of AuNC-Cu2+ were studied. The results showed that the optimal pH value was4.0 (Fig. S11a) and that the optimal temperature was40 °C (Fig. S11b). The stability of ox2-TMB was also studied, and almost no differences were observed in the spectra after storage for24h (Fig. S11c). These data illustrated that the ox2-TMB solution was stable, which satisfied the time requirements for measurements in this system.

Under optimal experimental conditions, a quantitative analysis of H2O2was performed using AuNC-Cu2+ . The calibration plot for the absorbance of ox2-TMB at a wavelength of452nm against the logarithmic concentration of H2O2is shown in Fig. 4. The fitting formula is expressed as follows: Y = 3.040 -2.913/(1 + (X/32.188)^1.299) (R2 = 0.998), where X is the logarithmic concentration of H2O2, and Y is the absorbance at452nm. There was a good fit between the absorbance of ox2-TMB at452nm and the concentrations of H2O2ranging from0.78 -400 μ M, and the limit of detection (LOD) was0.21 μ M, as estimated by the mean of the blank plus three times the standard deviation. The detection range and LOD of this method were greater than those with AuNCs as the catalytic material. AuNCs exhibited a detection range from5to1000 μ M with an LOD of0.61 μ M for H2O2detection (Fig. S12).

Fig. 4The dose-response curves for H2O2detection using AuNC-Cu2+ , between the absorbance at452nm against the logarithmic concentration of H2O2. Reaction conditions: AuNC-Cu2+ , 0.5mg·mL -1 ; TMB, 0.1mg·mL -1 ; and BR buffer (pH4.0). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements

# Glucose detection using AuNC-Cu@keratin


Based on the peroxidase-like activity of AuNC-Cu2+ , a colorimetric detection system for glucose analysis was designed. The glucose concentration was determined using AuNC-Cu2+ combined with GOx, because H2O2is the main product of Gox-catalyzed glucose oxidation. To evaluate the selectivity of our glucose test, control experiments were conducted with other saccharides and biomolecules, including fructose, saccharose, maltose, D-Glucitol, Dopamine at concentration of100 μ mol·L -1and Glycine, human serum albumin (HSA) at concentration of0.5mg·mL -1 . AuNC-Cu2+ system shows high selectivity towards glucose detection by monitoring the absorbance change upon addition of various glucose analogues (Fig. 5a). But some biothiols such as cysteine have

Fig. 5a The selectivity of AuNC-Cu2+ for100 μ mol·L -1glucose in the presence of100 μ mol·L -1fructose, saccharose, maltose, D-Glucitol, Dopamine and0.5mg·L -1Glycine and HSA. b The dose-response curves for glucose detection ( b ) using AuNC-Cu2+ , between the absorbance at452nm against the logarithmic concentration of H2O2and glucose. Reaction conditions: AuNC-Cu2+ , 0.5mg·mL -1 ; GOx, 2mg·mL -1 ; TMB, 0.1mg·mL -1 ; and BR buffer (pH4.0)

an impact on the reliability of AuNC-Cu2+ system due to their interaction with copper ions. Fortunately, most of the cysteine present in the serum is in the form of protein-complex. Thus, cysteine has no effect on the reliability of AuNC-Cu2+ system in serum sample (Fig. S13). Figure5b shows the calibration plot for glucose detection obtained at pH4and37 °C. The fitting formula is expressed as follows: Y = 3.500 -3.336/(1 + (X/131.197)^0.895) (R2 = 0.994), where X is the concentration of glucose, and Y is the absorbance at452nm. AuNCCu2+ exhibited a detection range from1.56to800 μ Mwith an LOD of0.61 μ Mfor glucose detection. As listed in Table S2, the sensitivity of our method was greater than those of most previously reported methods. To demonstrate the feasibility of the AuNC-Cu2+ probe-based colorimetric detection system, the determination of glucose levels in serum was performed. The recoveries ranged from94.6% to96.7%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than0.41% (Table S3). All of these data indicate that the colorimetric method based on AuNC-Cu2+ has great potential for detecting glucose in biological samples.


# Conclusions


Cu2+ can greatly enhance the peroxidase-like catalytic activity and stability of AuNCs through noncovalent d10-d10metallophilic interactions. In terms of enhancing catalytic activity, AuNC-Cu2+ displayed improved sensitivity for H2O2detection compared with AuNCs. Moreover, the catalytic activity of AuNC-Cu2+ remained essentially unchanged after two months. Based on these advantages of AuNC-Cu2+ , a sensing platform for the highly sensitive colorimetric detection of glucose was constructed. The method had good specificity and showed satisfying results for the detection of glucose in serum samples.

Fig. S4 (a) The time stability of AuNCs without any treatment before (line2and4) and after (line1and3) 7days. Reaction conditions: AuNCs, 0.5mg·mL -1 ; TMB, 0.1mg·mL -1 ; BR buffer (pH4.0); H2O2, 1000 μM (line1and2) or0 μM (line3and4). The peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs without any treatment showed an increasingly strong background signal after7days. (b and c) Effects of different storage methods on the peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs in the presence (b) and absence (c) of1000 μM H2O2. Black pillar: AuNCs were stored for1day. Blue pillar: AuNCs were stored for7days. Storage methods: (1) neutralize to pH7with a BR buffer; (2) purification by size exclusion chromatography; and (3) dialysis for12hours. Although purification methods2and3provided good stability, the catalytic efficiency of AuNCs was  greatly  reduced.
